text: One of our Cleveland Clinic Florida colorectal alumni, Dr. André Dore, professor of surgery at University Hospitals in Louvain, Belgium, and the innovator of the laparoscopic ventral rectopexy is going to speak to us about minimally invasive treatment of rectal prolapse. André, we're ready for you. Good morning, Steve. Hi, Steve. I hope you can hear me clear, and it's an honor to participate in this center, in this program. So here it comes. I'm going to address laparoscopic ventromesh rectopexy for rectoprolapse syndromes and open a little bit of the discussion so far. Next slide, please. So it's important that we do have some understanding about the disease and rather than in the former times where we clearly focused only on overt rectal prolapse, today we consider it as a kind of a spectrum and you have those patients with internal prolapses with obvious some clinical problems as obstructive defecation and fecal incontinence and we're going to address that and those combined prolapses as you can see with a rectal seal and interception or a huge enterocele that also protrudes through the anal rectal ring. Next. Next slide. So when we look to the age distribution, and this gives us a little bit of the flair of the disease, majority of female patients, ninety percent, ten percent are male patients, but you have like an early peak at the twenties here, and those are the patients who have clearly probably a congenital weakness of support of the rectum, Or those are the people who really have a problem with a chronic straining disorder and as an end result, they breach it open and they develop prolapses. When you go on the age axis and the other spectrum, you will dose the elderly people with a complete weakened denervated pelvic floor, and those are the prolapses where you see combinations with the prolapses of the middle and anterior pelvic compartment.

The video shows a young female patient with rectal prolapse, highlighting the deep Douglas fold and normal support of the middle pelvic compartment. The image transitions to show a combined prolapse with the sigmoid colon protruding, alongside a total rectal prolapse with uterine prolapse visible on the right side of the screen.

an important clinical snapshot how we treat rectal prolapses and Dr. Wexner was a co-author in this work done. And so we looked at the preferred treatment options in the United States and compared that to continental Europe and the UK. And certainly when you look, there is still in favor the resection rectopexy and in Europe we do the ventral mesh rectopexy and next slide please but when we look to the external prolapse in frail and elderly people There, we all agree that there is still a place for the perineal techniques. About eighty-two percent of all surgeons perform a kind of perineal technique in the old and frail people. But again, when you look to the younger generations with external prolapses, the classical prolapses in a fit patient, then you see the differences between Europe and North America. And on top of that, when we look to internal prolapse, this is only occasionally operated on in North America, but there is increasing interest to position the lab ventral rectopexy also for internal prolapses in Europe. Next slide. So, and although we are able to control the anatomic defect, again, what's the problem with rectal prolapse is often the poor functional result. And this becomes more apparent, I guess, and fuels the whole discussion when we shift then to internal prolapses. Next slide. When we look to the problem of constipation after rectopexy, and I guess this is very important, and of course you can induce a stenosis and a mechanical obstruction due to the mesh position. Of course, some will argue you should take out a redundant sigmoid colon, but most of patients with prolapse do have a redundant sigmoid colon. It's our belief, and this is backed by some data, it's that by doing a classical rectopexy technique, you induce autonomic denervation because in classical mesh rectopexy, you intensively, you completely mobilize the rectum and you will hook it up at a higher level. And next slide. In this elegant study performed in the Netherlands by Mullen, you can see this is the effect of doing rectal mobilization on colonic function. And you can see when you mobilize the rectum, you increase the colon transit time. And this is due to the segment tree. So in segment tree, you induce an inertia, and this is increased by a factor of two point five. Next slide. And as such, this provides and gives you some post hoc rationale why the Friedmann-Goldberg works, because you extensively mobilize the rectum, you hook it up with a suture pexy here, and then you resect the sigmoid colon, and it looks like that. So rather because the Friedmann-Golberg was invented or was started to reduce the number of recurrences, here on top you have the post hoc rationale, why it functionally works. Next slide.

The video shows a defecating proctogram or dynamic MRI revealing significant descent of the middle pelvic compartment, with the small bowel protruding through the anal ring during straining in a patient with total rectal prolapse. The laparoscopic ventral mesh rectopexy technique is illustrated, involving dissection within the rectovaginal septum, suturing to the anterior aspect of the rectum, and attachment to the sacral promontory to inhibit intussusception and correct middle compartment descent.

The surgeon lifts the small bowel out of the pelvis, revealing a deep prolapse. The sacral uterine ligaments are visible, and the ureter is identified as the peritoneum is opened along the sacral promontory. The left iliac vein is noted nearby. The peritoneum is incised, and the cul-de-sac is redressed to reduce the prolapse. A tunnel is created behind the vagina, with the assistant lifting the vagina for better visualization. A polypropylene mesh is positioned and tailored as needed, with the anterior aspect of the rectum sutured to the mesh to prevent further intussusception. The mesh is fixed to the sacral promontory, avoiding the hypogastric nerve. An additional suture is placed to prevent higher intussusception, and the peritoneal flaps are closed over the mesh, completing the procedure.

Okay, so maybe before we go on to some of the results, a beautiful video, and we'll talk about the technique, but I just want to back up a moment if we might, and Maybe turn to one of the other centers. Tell us, what is your pre-op evaluation in two scenarios? Firstly, you have a patient who comes in with full thickness rectal prolapse. What is your evaluation of that patient prior to surgery? So I think that, you know, these are patients, older patients, we make sure they have their recent screening colonoscopy. We evaluate the pelvic floor oftentimes with a pelvic MRI dynamic test to see if there's concomitant anterior prolapse. And then we go on to discuss both medical assessment of appropriate abdominal versus parietal approaches. So any physiology studies thrown in? There isn't any reason to do anything, manometry, EMG, defragments, any point in any of that, MRI? You can ask Ben next to you because he may know the answers. dynamic MRI pretty much will tell us you know the physiology to see if there's any internal interception or or any other concomitant pelvic floor dysfunction obviously see if there's any underlying fecal incontinence with the patient as part of the history constipation is part of the history straining is part of the history and also just the age of the patient as well to see what type of repair you might want to consider all kind of goes into the picture. Obviously, like you said, a recent colonoscopy and to rule out any other etiologies for the prolapse mechanical. Now, take the scenario of the patient who has intussusception. Let's say, you know, the patient gives symptoms of incomplete evacuation, some difficult evacuation, maybe during your office endoscopy you noted some intussusception. Do you do anything differently in that patient? And they have full thickness rectal prolapse as well? No, no, no, not full thickness, just rectoanal intussusception, either by suspicion or by endoscopy. Do you do a different evaluation, or is it the same in that patient? Yeah, I think I would confirm with MRI defecography or just anorectal physiology. Yeah, and I think that's the time where anal rectal menometry will give you some sense of, you know, paradoxical contraction, the puborectalis, give you some other senses of perhaps, you know, concomitant pelvic floor dysmotility with associated role of pelvic floor physical therapy to help improve, you know, anticipated functional outcome. You know, before rushing to a prolapse repair for interactive prolapse, I would probably stress more of a physiological or physical therapy approach to try to retrain muscles and get better function. And if it comes to internal rectal prolapse repair, then at least you're maximizing your chance of success from a functional perspective.

Okay, thanks very much. Let's go back to Louvain, Belgium, and ask Professor Dror, what is your evaluation in those two settings? Just to tell us, before you've decided on laparoscopic ventral rectopexy, either for prolapse or for interception, what's your preferred evaluation? Okay, so for external rectal prolapse... We ask them to have a full colonoscopy and of course we're going to look to defecograms, also to rule out what's happening in the middle and anterior compartment. to really have a complete picture before we embark on restoration of the prolapse. Of course, it's completely different with the patient coming in with obstructive defecation syndrome. In those, we do, on top of functional defecograms, we do a balloon evacuation test and also evacuation manometry. So we look whether they got this energy or not. And then when we see this energy, we're going to try to fix that first with biofeedback training. And it's always difficult to... to find out whether it's the internal prolapse itself, which you can clearly visualize on the defecography, or the functional deficit that causes a majority of the problems. So once we had them gone through biofeedback and we see some improvement on balloon evacuation tests or manometries when they void the rectum so that they get more appropriate expulsion tests, then probably we're going to give in a subgroup of those patients also Ventral Mesh Rectal Plexi. Okay, thanks. That's a very good explanation. I appreciate it. I want to turn for a moment, if we could, to Imperial College in London and see if either Ara Darzi or Paris Tekas... Nope. So who's the victim here? You guys have a big robotic experience. Tell us what you do for a rectal prolapse case. Is it the European standard laparoscopic fractal rectopexy? Is it robotic? Is it some other form of surgery? Preferred treatment for prolapse. Let's say in a fit, forty-five year old woman. Hi, I'm James Kinross. I'm one of the attendings here at Imperial. We don't routinely deploy robotic surgery for this. There are some centers in the UK who will do it as standard, but these are in the minority. A laparoscopic approach would absolutely be the standard approach, particularly in the patient of the demographic you've described. And how about, as Andre showed in his sort of algorithm, The elderly patient, so elderly unfit patient, he mentioned that there are two options, the Delorme, which at one point was the most popular in the survey from the Association of Colorectology of Great Britain and Ireland, and then the perineal rectosigmoidectomy, which Andre mentioned has certain advantages. What's your preferred approach these days? Well, I think in our institution, we still use the denomes as our go-to operation, really, in elderly people with a high comorbidity and high risk. Do you want to comment, Alex, on that at all? I think... Hi, I'm Alex Longwood. I'm an endometriosis surgeon director here at Imperial. I think as the PROSPER trial showed, the UK... experience and practice really is still largely based on perineal procedures, particularly in older patients. So they still hold sway in an older patient. Okay. Thanks a lot. Let's go back to Louvain, Belgium, and Professor Doerr can continue with the presentation, please, and then we'll pause again and ask some more questions shortly. Thank you.

So here you see our indications. It's external rectal prolapse, which is evident, and then the high-grade internal rectal prolapses, especially those with fecal incontinence, more controversial with obstructive defecation, and then those cases with rectocele with high-grade interception and enterocytes with high-grade interception. Next slide. So here you see our case series, about five hundred patients, large majority were female, mean age, fifty years. Again, you see some very young people and very old patients there. Forty percent were external rectal prolapses. And then you see the whole descent here, rectocele and entrocele and internal rectal prolapse, twenty-five percent. And then, forty percent for internal rectal prolapse with obstructive defecation or with incontinence. And we added the perineotomy in one hundred of those cases, but we are coming back from that approach because we have seen an increase of mesh erosions to the distal part of the vagina, especially in that subgroup of technical adjunct. Next slide. So when we regard to the safety profile, it's quite easy to perform. We had very few conversions. But I mentioned the left iliac vein is very close. In one patient, we had to convert because we had a severe bleeding at the side of the left iliac vein. If we had any mortality, progressively median hospital stay is going down and now we do it on one night stay and even some cases in day care procedures in hospital morbidity is very limited. Next slide. So it's important that we look to the long-term outcome I will address recurrences, functional outcome, fecal incontinence, constipation, and mesh-related septic complications. Next slide. And indeed we do have seen some recurrences and the longer you wait and you observe you see a slight increase there. So we looked to the group of patients with external rectal prolapse and this is a combined effort with a hospital from the Netherlands. We piled up two hundred forty two patients And at ten years interval, we do have a recurrence rate of eight point two percent. Next slide. And here you can see the Kaplan-Meyer estimates of recurrences. And this matches the results from more classical mesh rectopexy. So although we limited the amount of this section, you can see that the recurrence free survival is equal. Next slide. And this is important that we also look whether if you restore the static pelvic anatomy, whether we see a functional improvement on incontinence and constipation. Next slide. As I mentioned already, due to the prolapse itself, you get the mechanical stretching of the sphincter complex. There are also other mechanisms that will lead to fecal incontinence, like pudendal neuropathy, impaired rectal adaptation to distension, and impaired rectoanal motility. And next slide, when we looked to our pilot series of patients, you clearly can see that the median Wexner score was thirteen, with some patients clearly complete incontinence when they showed up for rectal prolapse repair. And you can see that postoperatively, you significantly increase the likelihood of continence. Although some, as you can see here, three patients remained completely incontinent. But we found an improvement, a significant improvement in ninety percent of our patients. Next slide. Again, when we go to the cohort analysis, and here we look to nine hundred twenty patients, and this is now published in Annals of Surgery, you see that a grade four incontinence was present preoperatively in thirty-seven percent of patients, and this drops to eleven percent, which is significantly. Next slide.

It's good to acknowledge that once you get the improvement early on, you see this remains stable over time. So once you add five-year follow-up or ten years post-operative follow-up, we see the same figures. So in some way, you can prevent further deterioration of continence in patients with prolapse. Next slide. And this is very important then when you look to a group of patients who were operated on not with overt rectal prolapse but with intra-anal rectal interception, so the deep endo-anal prolapses and incontinence, you see just the same improvement in that subgroup of patients. Next slide. And this brings us then, and this is certainly open for discussion, to a kind of algorithm that in those patients with high-grade interception and incontinence, probably you first should fix the interception and wait for the recovery. And only in those patients who have a persistent incontinence then embark to sacral nerve stimulation. But of course, there should be, in the future, a head-to-head comparison where you can randomize patients with high-grade interception to a lab-ventral mesh rectopexy and sacral nerve stimulation. Of course, next slide, we do know that there are some predictors of... persistence of incontinence. This is the patient's average age. Of course, the older the patients, the more risk you have that they remain incontinent, symptom duration, and also those patients who have preoperative urinary incontinence. Let's just interject here two things. Firstly, you showed a little algorithm there that included Also, nerve stimulation as an option in this group. And I'm curious just to get another view on the basic treatment algorithm again and how the incontinence is treated, whether first addressing the incontinence or first addressing the intussusception. If that's the case, let's go to IRCAD in Strasbourg, France, and perhaps either Jacques or Joël is there. Somebody might take a stab at this. Your preferred algorithm, is it what André discussed or another one? Hello, I'm Didier Muter from Strasbourg. Joël Leroy is not here. He's traveling somewhere in the world. So we do... We can have an answer from one of our colorectal fellows here in Strasbourg. Good morning or good afternoon. Thank you for the nice presentation. We have not so many experience with sacral nerve stimulation, but this might be a very promising treatment for patients that have a persistent incontinence may be caused by pudendal nerve neuropathy. So, indeed, we think this is a very interesting future treatment, and we might embark on these kinds of treatments as well in the future. Thank you. Thank you very much, and I wish you all safe travels. Can we go to Anne Arundel Medical Center, please? We have a question from Anne Arundel that they'd like to pose. Morning, Adrian. Hey, good morning, Steve. Actually, the question, I'm very much appreciating the presentation, but from my own naive perspective, I don't do this surgery, but as one who does a lot of hernia and foregut surgery, I'm intrigued by the placement of this mesh, and the professor said there's been one vaginal erosion. I mean, it In the hernia and foregrip world, I mean, it's anathema to us to put polypropylene or polyester anywhere close to viscera or around the esophagus or anything. How long, you know, what's your explanation, I guess, for the small number of erosions when you're putting it straight on rectum and vagina? It's remarkable to me the esophagus wouldn't tolerate that for a minute. And again, on abdominal wall reconstructions, we'd never consider exposing polypropylene that way. I'm just intrigued, and I'm intrigued that this doesn't become a complication. Thanks a lot, Adrian. So let's turn back to Luvan Belgium and Andre.

Why don't you take that question? Yeah, I can address that. I guess, of course, you don't put a mesh on the colon, but you rather put a mesh in front of the muscular coat of the rectum, and there the rectal coat is quite a different texture than a colon. Also, you try to avoid to have any kinking of the mesh so that it remains flat. And when we do the dissection, we leave all remainder fatty tissue, so we go behind the nobilis fascia. So the nobilis fascia, or the remainders of that, remain to the vaginal side. So you still have like a layer then between the mesh and the dorsal vagina. Okay, so when we looked into constipation, and this was our cohort, our first study, a pilot study, We quoted patients as having constipation in fifty six percent and we used a validated questionnaires for that and that postoperatively dropped to twenty six percent. Next slide. And here you clearly can see that we corrected obstructed defecation. And that was quite an interesting finding that probably due to the position of the mesh into the rectovaginal septum, you reinforce the rectovaginal septum and that could help the erectile evacuation. And we saw very few new onset obstructive defecations. Next slide. This then has been seen also in other series. Next slide, please. And you can see that about seventy percent of patients do see a clear improvement of obstructive defecation and that new onset or worsening of obstructive defecation is seldom. Next slide. But of course constipation is quite complex and you can have defecation disorders on top of that some overlap with slow transit constipation, the IBS group of patients and of course you should rule out organic or secondary constipations. So this makes it very important that you make an appropriate patient selection. Next slide. And this, of course, started the whole discussion, and it was raised by the Oxford group that said, okay, now we have a tool also to operate on patients with internal rectal prolapse, But of course, Sir Lorberg was very critical on that in colorectal disease in two thousand and fourteen that it's really time for a critical appraisal and we shouldn't overrate, I guess, the role of operating on patients of internal prolapse and obstructive defecation. Next slide. It was again the group of Oxford who looked into detail in quite a number of patients, with internal rectal prolapse. Next slide. And also another group from the UK. And you clearly can see that they saw an important improvement in constipation scores, not only in patients with total rectal prolapse, but also in patients with rectal seals and intussusception and obstructive defecation syndrome. Next slide. and the group from Nantes looked to their patients operated on for complex rectal seals and you clearly can see that the more you have constipation before the operation, the more the likelihood of having any benefit out of that. Next slide. When we looked into our cohort data again, in the pre-op setting, fifty-four percent of all patients were constipated and that dropped to sixteen percent. And you can see that new onset constipation is very seldom. Next slide. This brings us to like an overall figure that about seventy percent of patients do have an improvement. But of course, you can also look to that in a reversed way and say, OK, it only works in seventy percent. So in thirty percent of patients, it doesn't do anything. So I'd leave it again open for discussion.

I think that's an excellent point to take home. Maybe we can turn to one of the other senators we haven't asked, and Sharet Zedek in Israel, and ask is this the preferred approach? Good afternoon, gentlemen. Where does the laparoscopic ventral rectopexy fall for your treatment of prolapse? Is it the preferred treatment for younger patients? And if so, is it also the preferred treatment in the older group, or do you still use perineal approaches? Well, we use perineal approaches seldom for older people with low functional capacity. And for younger fit people, we always use the resection rectopexy. like old school, but I was very impressed with the results of Professor Dewar. Although I have a couple of questions if he can explain to us the physiology of improving constipation after such a procedure because looking at this technique, it looks like that this mesh is actually making the sigmoid rectal angle more acute. and I would expect that that may cause a problem in terms of colon motility. So I would be curious to know what is the explanation of improving constipation with this technique. The other question that I have is whether diverticulosis, which is a very common finding in such patients. Is this a contraindication for the procedure? Is it worrisome that diverticula are in very close vicinity to foreign material? What are your thoughts about that? Excellent question. Thank you. So we looked to the mechanism in which or how this procedure would work for obstructification. one way is the positioning of the mesh because you reinforce the rectovaginal septum and a lot of those patients also have like a tendency to have a rectocele and some of the patients with prolapse also digitate and reinforce digitally the vagina to evacuate the rectum so by the unique position of the mesh You reinforce, so you make the anterior aspect of the rectum more rigid. Also, by reducing the interception, it helps the patients to avoid the rectum. And we did colonic transit time, but I hadn't the time to look, to show it today. but we clearly could see a significant decrease in colonic transit time, especially in the third segment. So, indeed, we could prove that we saw an increased or a more rapid evacuation of the rectum. We haven't seen any impact on rectal capacity by the positioning of the mesh. So in the second part of your question is very interesting because indeed when we do have a patient with a really diverticular colon on top of prolapse, in those patients we favor to do a resection rectopexy. So indeed, there is a clear issue there that we don't, especially when the patient has a history of like diverticular disease, then we would favor a resection rectopexy. Thanks, André. Why don't we leave it with you to just wrap up because we're at the end of our time and we'll leave you for the last couple of words. Okay, then we go further with slides. Next slide, please. Next slide. And I guess it has been addressed so far. And the question is, of course, whether you can use this for the whole problem of the vaginal mesh is coming into play here. But there is complete difference in the figures when we look to septic complications when you do the applications through the scope or whether you do a transfer channel approach. Next slide. Here you clearly can see when you look to all figures that mesh erosions are very seldom. The majority were when we did it with a combined perineotomy. And if you avoid that, you still can go lower than that. And chronic mesh infections and fistula are extremely seldom. Next slide. This is completely different from transfer general prolapse repairs where you see erosions in about eleven percent already within twelve months. Next slide. When you look to sacral colpopexy there it drops already to four point seven percent and in our series we are below two percent. Next slide. When it did occur, as you can see here in the distal vagina, we pre-prepared the vagina with oestrogel and then we make an incision and core it out and close the vagina on top. Next slide. there certainly is some discussion going on whether we need a permanent scaffold and I do believe we do need and of course the biological meshes are not all the same here you see the permacol not behaving like a classical biological mesh next slide next slide please And here you can see that there is an increase of recurrences already in the limited literature when you look to biological. Next slide. And this was clearly the findings in our urogynecology practice. As you can see here, the synthetic and biological, a significant difference in recurrence rate. So this will be, of course, the trade-off of the whole discussion. What is the number of recurrences you do accept? Next slide, an ultimate slide for this meeting. So here you can see surgery for rectal prolapse. We should restore the anatomy. We should look to incontinence and obstructification. When we look to recurrence rates, there the abdominal procedures are better than the perineal. When we look to the functional outcome, there the abdominal procedures are the winners. But the caveat is, of course, nuance at constipation. Then when we look to invasiveness, there the abdominal procedures are more invasive than the perineal procedures. Therefore, next slide, we should favor... the laparoscopic approach to reduce invasiveness. We should do rectopexy to reduce the number of recurrences. And of course, we should position the mesh in our mindset at the ventral aspect to favor the functional outcome. And thank you for being with me this morning. Thanks, Andre, for a phenomenal presentation. Really well done, very compelling, convincing data, and represents certainly, you know, seventeen years now of working on this procedure and bringing it through the world.
